review_text: Like others, having a complete headache getting a refund due to the war. Everytime you speak to someone on Facebook (because its impossible to call them or use their live chat), you get a different answer as to what is going on. They are clearly working off a script because they cannot answer very specific basic questions. You also cannot speak to anyone in the mythical refunds team so there is no hope of a lot of us ever seeing our hard earned money again. Worst customer service ever. Due to the war situation in the Middle East, I cancelled my flights on April 1st. The hotel processed the refund immediately. Emirates, however, has not returned approximately 3000 EUR even after more than a month. On March 23rd, I was clearly informed that the refund would take up to 15 working days. That timeline has long passed. What followed has been particularly disappointing: multiple contacts with customer service, inconsistent information, and explanations that simply do not hold up — including being told I had not provided bank details (which is incorrect), followed by requests for “understanding” due to a high volume of refunds. One could perhaps understand delays. What is much harder to understand is the absence of transparency, accountability, and any sense of urgency in resolving a straightforward refund case.  For a company that positions itself as a leader in global aviation, this experience is surprising and not in a good way. At this point, I would hesitate to recommen

I recently booked a flight with Emirates from Lisbon to Bali (booking reference LCFKW6). The original itinerary had a short 1h30 connection in Dubai, with a total travel time of around 19 hours. Prior to departure, we were only notified of a minor delay. At no point were we informed that this would result in a missed connection. Only at check-in, approximately 2.5 hours before departure, the passenger was told the onward flight had been changed, resulting in an 18-hour layover in Dubai and a total journey time of approximately 36 hours. Had this been communicated earlier, alternative arrangements could have been made. We were instructed to seek assistance at the transfer desk in Dubai, but no hotel, lounge access, or meaningful support was provided despite the extended layover. This represents both a failure of communication and a lack of duty of care. The significantly extended transit time also created additional stress and concern, as a short connection had been specifically chosen

I conclude that they are truly worse than a budget carrier. I received a horrendous customer service on-board flight by a cabin supervisor who was unable to manage a difficult passenger sitting on the other end of my row who was trying to take a nap at 5pm, so he came up to me to command me to close the window shades. I explained to him I was feeling unwell and wanted to leave it slightly open (1/4 open and 3/4 closed). He refused! He said that there’s another passenger across the aisle that is complaining and I should comply with his request. Only when I ask him if I fainted on board, would he be responsible, did he say it was my right to leave the window shades open and he will handle it with that lady. Many people had their window shades opened. Mine was three quarter 3/4 closed. Does it make sense that instead of forcing me, he should have given her an eye mask instead?  Inefficiency of Customer Service Team with replies almost only once a month. Upon arrival, I noticed damage made

Flew in business class from Hong Kong to Bangkok on the 20th March. The lounge at Hong kong airport was nowhere near to Emirates usual standard. The aircraft looked very dated indeed with sockets for the TV not working and neither were the USB sockets for charging our phones. The seats were extremely uncomfortable for a business class flight Food was average at best and service was also very slow. A passenger who spent most of the journey at the on-board bar was drunk when he returned to his seat. Even although the same passenger became rather loud and was also stood up in the aisle just after landing the crew did nothing about the passengers behaviour. This is not acceptable. I am very disappointed with Emirates. I booked my tickets almost a year before travelling as I like to carefully select my seat and more importantly, the type of aircraft I will be travelling on. Emirates changed the second half of the inbound journey from an A380(which I had booked) to a 777-300. They did not even inform me. I found out when I was looking something up, which resulted in me phoning them and writing to them to ask to move me back to any A380 going into any London airport at any time that day. My e-mail, I never got a reply to, and the telephone conversation resulted in them saying I could pay an additional £400,- for the honour of changing my ticket due to what they had done, not me. They later put a disclaimer on the booking saying “mixed fleet”. The 777-300 was an old and noisy aircraft with an illuminated Non Smoking sign above my head, and nowhere to put my bag anywhere near me. To me, it felt more like a Premium Economy than business class. I have flown with Emirat

Business Check-in was a bit of a trek from the metro station, but I appreciated the priority tags and also my ski bag being taken from the check-in counter, rather than me having to drag it to the other side of the terminal. Emirates have organised their lounges so they're above the gate you leave from. The lounge had a good array of foods for breakfast. I was pretty annoyed that they didn't give us a jetbridge to board, but bus instead.  On board the crew spoilt us, meals were beautifully presented and they tasted great and there was an array of snacks available. The seats were not the refurbished seats, but were still very comfortable. The cabin was only about 1/2 full, so plenty of room to move around. We were delayed in immigration at Denpasar due to me not following the correct procedure with the airport tax and our bags were waiting for us at the carousel. I'll definitely give Emirates another go. Aircraft Boeing 777 Type Of Traveller Family Leisure Seat Type Business Class Route

I sometimes wonder of the veracity of some over praiseworthy reviews, or were we just unlucky? Our our Manchester to Dubai flight business class was atrocious, we were stuck in the middle two of three centre business class seats - most airlines premium economy is better than this, and the plane was tired and fixtures worn and tatty. Service was abrupt and staff disappeared into the kitchens and stayed there for most of the flight. The food was okay, at BA standard from my recent trips with them. Luckily our transfer in Dubai was fairly brief, because it is an awful place. Our trip in April to Thailand is with Qatar Airways, who are a very long way ahead of Emirates in my experience - a bit like comparing Waitrose with Lidl. Check in at Colombo was quick and there is a priority immigration queue at Colombo that saves a bit of time. Emirates Lounge is of a decent size and is comfortable, with a reasonable choice of food. Flight to Dubai was on a refurbished B777 with the new seating, very comfortable. We chose the smoked salmon starter and roast chicken as the main, genuinely nice. Flight had Wi-Fi onboard but not the latest StarLink and service was a bit unstable. Due to congestion we were 45 minutes late into Dubai. This meant a very tight transit in Dubai. At the time of booking our trip the leg from Dubai to London Gatwick was on an A380, unfortunately Emirates changed the aircraft to a B777 two weeks before travel but did not have the courtesy to advise the passengers, This aircraft was the older config B777 (some 20 years old) with the 3 seats in the middle business class. Whilst I understand Emirates right to change the aircraft type, this was a big step down compared with our expectation of comfort

Check in at LGW was quick and easy. Flight to Dubai was on an A380, which we prefer to the B777. My wife and I had seats 9E and 9F, which are the middle ‘honeymoon’ seats!. Service was very slick with a good cabin crew. We both chose the beef fillet as the main course, which was tender and juicy. Flight was uneventful with no turbulence. Toilets kept clean and nice to enjoy the bar at the rear of the cabin. Flight landed ahead of schedule. Transit through Dubai was easy with minimal queuing at Transit security. Connecting flight from Dubai to Colombo was on a refurbished B777. We had seats 5E and 5F, very comfortable and the overall ambience on the cabin was very nice. Aircraft was fitted with StarLink so super stable and free Wi-Fi available, very impressive. Flight was only 4 hours, so we didn’t bother with the cooked breakfast offered, so just had the fruits and pastries prior to landing on Colombo. Flight arrived slightly ahead of schedule, immigration was quick and the bags were a

Extremely disappointing experience on Emirates EK416 (Dubai to Sydney, 06 Feb — 13h 50m flight). Despite booking my family under a single booking reference, Emirates split us across different rows, seating my wife and one child separately from me and my other child. Another passenger was placed in our original aisle seats, forcing our family apart on a nearly 14-hour long-haul flight. It looks like Emirates is deliberately separating families to push passengers into paying extra for seat reservations. This is unethical, customer-unfriendly, and unacceptable, especially when traveling with children. Family seating should be a basic right, not a paid privilege. This experience has seriously damaged my trust in Emirates. I was very disappointed with my recent experience flying with Emirates. The pre-boarding process was extremely disorganized. Cabin crew appeared uncoordinated and were running around without clear direction, which set a poor tone from the start of the journey. During meal service, my pre-requested vegetarian meal, which I had arranged several weeks prior to the flight, was not provided. This is a basic expectation on long-haul flights and reflects poorly on the airline’s attention to detail and passenger needs. The speed and efficiency of service throughout the flight was also subpar. Service was slow, inconsistent, and highlighted the lack of organization among staff. Additionally, Emirates often promotes its ICE entertainment system as industry leading, but I found the movie and TV selection to be outdated and underwhelming. Overall, this flight fell well below expectations. I would only consider flying with Emirates again if there were no other options, as there are several airlines

This review was being written while passengers are still on a bus, driving for more than ten minutes to reach the aircraft. Over multiple flights, a clear and uncomfortable pattern has emerged: routes serving certain countries are repeatedly assigned remote gates with bus boarding, while flights to other destinations receive jet bridges. When this happens again and again, it no longer feels like an operational coincidence — it feels like unequal treatment based on route, and by extension, the passengers traveling on it. Adding to the frustration is the behavior of some ground staff. Instead of empathy or professionalism, passengers are often met with impatience and dismissive attitudes, which only reinforces the feeling of being treated as less important.  Equal standards should apply to all passengers, regardless of where they are flying to. Respect, fairness, and consistency are not optional values. I had the worst experience with the Emirates baggage customer care team. My luggage was lost during travel, and instead of proper support, the compensation process was extremely disappointing. I lost around 12 kg of personal items including clothes, shoes, and essential belongings. Emirates is asking for original purchase receipts for compensation, which is not realistic. No one travels assuming their luggage will be lost and keeps receipts for everything they pack. The compensation offered was very low and not even close to the value of what I lost. Even buying a single pair of good shoes with it is difficult. On top of that, the process is extremely slow. It took almost a month just to get an update, and now the compensation payment is said to take another month. This entire process has been stressful, time-consuming, and disappointing. There was a long queue, even in business class — I was flying business. Boarding these enormous aircraft is always a challenge, as there are never enough ground staff or air bridges. Not to mention the economy passengers who must queue for nearly a hundred metres before reaching their seats. My previous flight was with Air Mauritius, a smaller airline, but there were only a handful of passengers in business class and I felt much more comfortable. Emirates, on the other hand, is really about the mass market. It doesn’t offer the same refined, intimate luxury experience I’ve had with some other airlines. The aircraft are always full, and overbooking is common — I’ve even been bumped to another flight before because of it. That said, the onboard service was perfectly fine. Emirates is widely praised — though not by me — and I find it somewhat overrated. It’s all about moving as many people as possible; the goal is clearly to fill those massive planes, and it shows. I recently flew with Emirates and had a mixed experience. The plane felt quite old and narrow compared to expectations, which made the journey less comfortable. Not very wheelchair-friendly overall, though some effort was made. The meals served were enjoyable, and the toilets were well maintained throughout the flight. The crew were friendly and patient, especially in accommodating my mother who was traveling in a wheelchair. However, their grooming standards could be improved — some did not appear as neat or polished, with hair not properly combed. Overall, while the food and cleanliness met expectations and the crew were kind, the aging aircraft, limited accessibility, and grooming standards left room for improvement.
